St George: Our Saint 18 April 2026 St George: Our Saint Archbishop Stephen writes this week's Credo column in The Times. Welcome from the Archbishop of York I serve as the 98th Archbishop of York, supporting the Diocese of York and the dioceses of the Northern Province of the Church of England. My priorities for the North What is Faith in the North? Faith in the North looks to encourage a movement of prayer, storytelling and church planting inspired by the Northern Saints. Find out more Pilgrimage Pilgrimage is not simply a walk; it is following a route that many others have walked before, where they have encountered the Holy on route to a holy place Learn more Thy Kingdom Come This year’s Thy Kingdom Come is all about the presence of God through His spirit. Join the global wave of prayer from 14-24 May 2026. Join the global wave of prayer Prayer for the Day Sunday, 3 May 2026 The Fifth Sunday of Easter Your browser does not support the audio element. Risen Christ, your wounds declare your love for the world and the wonder of your risen life: give us compassion and courage to risk ourselves for those we serve, to the glory of God the Father. Amen. Serving the Diocese of York and the dioceses of the Northern Province of the Church of England. Meet Stephen Cottrell Discover more about the 98th Archbishop of York, The Most Reverend Stephen Cottrell. The Archbishop’s team The Archbishop is supported by a dedicated team of advisers, administrators and clerical, technical and maintenance staff at... Previous Archbishops of York Stephen Cottrell is the 98th Archbishop of York to have served the church in the Northern Province. Ministry and role of the Archbishop The Northern Province The provinces of Canterbury and York reflect the beginnings of the structure of the Church of England The Anglican Communion The Archbishop is one of the Primates of the Anglican Communion representing the Church of England internationally. Diocese of York An overview of the Archbishop's work and ministry in the Diocese of York. York Minster York Minster is the seat of the Archbishop of York and the cathedral church of the Diocese and Province of York. Patronages Archbishop Stephen is patron to a number of charitable organisations, locally, nationally and internationally. Explore further Publications Stephen Cottrell has written more than 20 books on subjects such as evangelism, spirituality, and discipleship, as well as... pages.title: Faith in the North | Archbishop of York
headings: h2: What is Faith in the North?
h3: Renewal of prayer and discipleship
pages.text_excerpt: Faith in the North | Archbishop of York Skip to main content Archbishop of York Main navigation Search Close Menu Home The Archbishop About the Archbishop Ministry and role Meet Stephen Cottrell The Archbishop’s team Priorities About his priorities Faith in the North The North Prayer Evangelism News, speeches and sermons Bishopthorpe Palace About Bishopthorpe Palace History of Bishopthorpe Palace Seasons in the garden Prayer and worship Key links Safeguarding Contact us Other websites The Church of England Archbishop of Canterbury A Church Near You What are you looking for? Popular searches Faith in the North Faith in the North Breadcrumb Home Priorities Faith in the North Back Faith in the North Faith in the North emerged from two years of conversations about how to celebrate the 1400th anniversary of the baptism in York by Bishop Paulinus of King Edwin, the first King of Northumbria to profess the Christian faith, in 2027. In the lead-up to 2027, Faith in the North will encourage us to join together in prayer, help us share our stories of faith and our rich Christian heritage, and see 3,000 New Worshipping Communities across the North. Photo credit Office of the Archbishop of York What is Faith in the North? Faith in the North seeks to encourage a movement of prayer, storytelling and church planting inspired by the Northern Saints. It will provide resources for churches, schools and individuals to foster deeper connections with the Christian faith and renew missionary purpose. Renewal of prayer and discipleship More people praying the Lord’s Prayer and living lives shaped by the vision it presents. Children and young people knowing and engaging with the Lord’s Prayer as a pathway to faith. A prayer network for church planting and revitalisation across the region. More people discovering a relationship with God through resources for prayer. Renewal of storytelling People seeing themselves as part of the big story of Faith in the North through the centuries and encouraged and equipped to tell their story today. Children in schools learning the story of Faith in the North in ways which engage them in the Christian faith. Churches engaging with their story with fresh imagination and developing new creative approaches to mission. People of all ages encouraged to explore faith for themselves. Renewal of church planting Churches and new worshipping communities started because of our work. A shared sense of identity and purpose across the Northern Province of the Church of England. The Northern Province as a learning community for church planting. Photo credit Faith in the North Pilgrimage – the focus for 2026 Throughout 2026, churches of every size and context are invited to become Pilgrim Places – locations where visitors can pause, explore, and deepen their spiritual journey. Resources are available to help with this. Schools are also encouraged to help pupils think about pilgrimage and the Northern Saints, with resources to support, including a new song and church trail cards. Visit the Faith in the North website for more information and to access free resources. Prayer for the Day Sunday, 3 May 2026 The Fifth Sunday of Easter Your browser does not support the audio element. Risen Christ, your wounds declare your love for the world and the wonder of your risen life: give us compassion and courage to risk ourselves for those we serve, to the glory of God the Father. Amen. campaigns.title: What is Faith in the North?
campaigns.summary: Faith in the North seeks to encourage a movement of prayer, storytelling and church planting inspired by the Northern Saints. It will provide resources for churches, schools and individuals to foster deeper connections with the Christian faith and renew missionary purpose.

campaigns.title: Pilgrimage – the focus for 2026
campaigns.summary: Throughout 2026, churches of every size and context are invited to become Pilgrim Places – locations where visitors can pause, explore, and deepen their spiritual journey. Resources are available to help with this.

Alternatively, you can write to Office of the Archbishop of York, Bishopthorpe Palace, Bishopthorpe, York, YO23 2GE or call the switchboard during office hours (9am - 5pm) at 01904 707021. You may have a query which is answered on the FAQs . We value the letters and emails we receive, but due to the sheer volume, we are unable to read or reply to all of them. pages.text_excerpt: Accessibility statement | Archbishop of York Skip to main content Archbishop of York Main navigation Search Close Menu Home The Archbishop About the Archbishop Ministry and role Meet Stephen Cottrell The Archbishop’s team Priorities About his priorities Faith in the North The North Prayer Evangelism News, speeches and sermons Bishopthorpe Palace About Bishopthorpe Palace History of Bishopthorpe Palace Seasons in the garden Prayer and worship Key links Safeguarding Contact us Other websites The Church of England Archbishop of Canterbury A Church Near You What are you looking for? Popular searches Faith in the North Christian Hume/unsplash.com Breadcrumb Home Help using this website Accessibility statement Back Accessibility statement This website is designed to be as accessible to as many users as possible, and to meet or exceed current UK accessibility legislation. This accessibility statement applies to content published on the Archbishop of York’s website at archbishopofyork.org. It does not apply to content accessed via any subdomains or third-party websites, which may have separate accessibility statements. To ensure all visitors can use the website, these pages aim to meet WCAG 2.1 Level AA, the international standard for web accessibility. This includes: Navigating the website with just a keyboard or speech recognition tools, without the use of a mouse Navigating the website using a screen reader Using content that is structured, ordered and labelled appropriately and text which is simple to understand Including alternative text descriptions for images and providing non-visual alternatives where appropriate The inclusion of subtitles in any videos to ensure they are accessible to users who are deaf and hard of hearing AbilityNet has advice on making your device easier to use if you have a disability. Major operating systems – Microsoft , Apple , Android – also produce accessibility guidance for the device you are using. While this website strives to adhere to accepted accessibility and usability guidelines and standards, it is not always possible to do so across all areas of the site. We are always looking to improve the accessibility of this website. We regularly conduct accessibility audits, quality assurance and manual checks to identify areas for improvement. If you are having difficulty accessing any part of this website, please contact us . We aim to respond to your request within two working days. Last updated: 23 April 2026.
